text: This is a fifty nine year old female who is currently undergoing chemotherapy for endometrial cancer who had five day history of right upper quadrant abdominal pain period.
She had an ultrasound which showed a stone lodged in the neck of the gallbladder.
The video shows periduodenal and infundibular edema, with visible swelling around the duodenum and gallbladder infundibulum.
Definitely indicating acute cholecystitis here. I don't think there's any surprise here on that.
The surgeon works on the posterior side of the gallbladder, likely addressing the inflamed tissue due to acute cholecystitis.
The surgeon clears the surrounding tissue to bring the cystic artery into clear view.
The surgeon observes the cystic duct diving deep into the surgical field, inspecting its position relative to surrounding structures.
The surgeon works on the posterior side as the duct appears to dive deeper into the tissue.
The surgeon maneuvers to clear the artery from the surgical field, revealing a large posterior artery branch.
The surgeon toggles between views, noting the lack of cystic duct illumination despite using indocyanine green dye.
The surgeon attempts to dissect the infundibulum of the gallbladder, which appears visually fused to the cystic duct at the exit point, making the dissection challenging.
The surgeon achieves the critical view of safety, revealing the inferior margin of the right hepatic lobe during the robotic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon observes the bifurcation of the posterior artery branch near the cystic plate, which appears highly vascular. Efforts are made to preserve all visible vessels as the branching is noted.

The video shows extensive pericholecystic fluid along the cystic plate, extending almost the entire length of the gallbladder. The gallbladder appears to be significantly intrahepatic, which may not be immediately apparent.
The surgeon observes the complete collapse of the cystic plate and nearly enters the gallbladder during the dissection.
The surgeon observes a fatty liver while noting the clear visibility of the cystic plate.
